After reviewing the provided material, I observe that none of the retrieved documents mention anything about "sparrow foot marks," nor does any reference link them to forensic determination of driver status after auto accidents. There is significant coverage of topics like gait analysis, footprint interpretation, forensic odontology, burn assessments, and trauma classifications—but nothing substantiates or validates the existence or utility of "sparrow foot marks" as a signifier for driver identification. Given that this term appears absent across all sources—even among detailed discussions around footprints and gait—the claim that such markings serve a valid forensic function in identifying drivers lacks empirical grounding. Therefore, despite earlier consensus suggesting Option C had no clinical merit, the current search confirms it independently via absence of validation in authoritative forensic texts. My conclusion aligns with prior judgment: sparrow foot marks are not supported by existing evidence and thus cannot reasonably distinguish driver from passenger.
\boxed{C}
